coredata.dc:title: Recent advances in environmental sample preparation: Nanomaterials, automation, and sustainable strategies

coredata.dc:description: The increasing occurrence of micropollutants and the need for their monitoring in environmental matrices have driven the demand for more efficient and accurate analytical methods. In particular, sample preparation procedures are essential for addressing the complexity of environmental samples while ensuring high sensitivity. To meet these demands, various nanomaterials (NMs) have been proposed as extractive phases for development of miniaturized sorbent-based extraction approaches. These NMs, ranging from carbon-based nanostructures and metal-based or metal oxide nanoparticles, to metal–organic composites, are at the forefront of innovation due to their exceptional surface areas, tunable properties, ease of functionalization and, in some cases, green production routes. On the other hand, the development of (semi)automated platforms has facilitated high-throughput and reproducible sample processing, significantly reducing the use of reagents, time, and labor. This review highlights the pivotal role of NMs and automation in advancing sample preparation methodologies over the past five years, in alignment with green sample preparation (GSP) guidelines. Additionally, the integration of emerging technologies, such as 3D printing, is discussed in the context of developing modern sample preparation methods. Recent contributions involving miniaturized platforms, green nanosorbents, and the integration of processing steps with automation, are evaluated for their potential to provide highly efficient, cost-effective solutions for monitoring micropollutants in environmental matrices.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The increasing occurrence of micropollutants and the need for their monitoring in environmental matrices have driven the demand for more efficient and accurate analytical methods. In particular, sample preparation procedures are essential for addressing the complexity of environmental samples while ensuring high sensitivity. To meet these demands, various nanomaterials (NMs) have been proposed as extractive phases for development of miniaturized sorbent-based extraction approaches. These NMs, ranging from carbon-based nanostructures and metal-based or metal oxide nanoparticles, to metal–organic composites, are at the forefront of innovation due to their exceptional surface areas, tunable properties, ease of functionalization and, in some cases, green production routes. On the other hand, the development of (semi)automated platforms has facilitated high-throughput and reproducible sample processing, significantly reducing the use of reagents, time, and labor. This review highlights the pivotal role of NMs and automation in advancing sample preparation methodologies over the past five years, in alignment with green sample preparation (GSP) guidelines. Additionally, the integration of emerging technologies, such as 3D printing, is discussed in the context of developing modern sample preparation methods. Recent contributions involving miniaturized platforms, green nanosorbents, and the integration of processing steps with automation, are evaluated for their potential to provide highly efficient, cost-effective solutions for monitoring micropollutants in environmental matrices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite significant advancements in recent decades, the quantification and accurate identification of analytes at trace concentration levels remain major challenges in many analytical methods. Therefore, the effectiveness of the clean-up and preconcentration steps during sample preparation is crucial to the overall success of the process. Besides, the elimination of sample preparation steps is practically unfeasible in environmental analysis due to the complexity of matrices, the presence of interferences, and the need for high-sensitivity determinations [1]. In this context, advances in sample preparation techniques focus on several key aspects, such as reducing time and costs to make the process more efficient and less labor-intensive, minimizing the use of hazardous solvents and reducing waste to develop safer and environmentally friendly procedures, automating and miniaturing steps to decrease solvent consumption and improve efficiency, and ensuring easy coupling with instruments, to enhance the total analytical process by improving the workflow and detection capabilities. Following these trends, several miniaturized extraction techniques have been proposed as alternatives to the classical methods, primarily based on liquid–liquid extraction (LLE) and solid-phase extraction (SPE) [2]. Single drop microextraction (SDME), dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction (DLLME), stir bar sorptive extraction (SBSE), dispersive solid-phase extraction (DSPE), magnetic solid-phase extraction (MSPE), solid-phase microextraction (SPME), microextraction by packed sorbent (MEPS), and micro-solid phase extraction (µSPE), among others, have been successfully implemented for environmental sample preparation, as reported in the literature [3–6]. Accordingly, the concept of green sample preparation (GSP) has gained attention as an extension of the Green Analytical Chemistry (GAC) requirements, aiming to contribute to the development of sustainable analytical methodologies while focusing on the main aspects of the sample treatment step [2,7]. The ten principles of GSP address the use of solvents, reagents and materials, energy demand, speed, waste generation, as well as the miniaturization, simplification and automation of systems and devices, while ensuring the analyst's safety. In recent decades, significant advancements have been made in the use of nanomaterials (NMs) as extractive phase in sorbent-based extraction (SBE) techniques. Their chemical composition, size distribution, morphology, and surface characteristics play a pivotal role in developing efficient and reproducible strategies with minimal solvent and reagent consumption [8,9]. Substantial efforts are also being directed toward the development of new synthesis routes that produce materials with well-defined properties while minimizing energy and reagent usage, as well as labor and time, thereby offering greener alternatives to traditional sorbents [10]. In this sense, the synergy between the wide variety of nanosorbents and vast diversity of SBE techniques provides a versatile toolkit for simple, cost-effective and environmentally friendly sample preparation.

Even in the case of miniaturized extraction systems, most of the approaches involve multiple steps (e.g., centrifugation, vortexing, drawn-and-drop cycles, stirring, and others), which often result in considerable analyst intervention. These steps increase the analysis time, complexity of procedures, and represent a potential source of systematic errors and random lack of precision of the analytical method. In this context, the automation of procedures has emerged to provide faster, simpler, and more environmentally friendly alternatives to traditional ones [11,12]. These procedures use developed systems based on different principles, such as flow, discrete (or batch), and programmable multi-commutation, which have been described in the literature [13,14]. Furthermore, multidisciplinary research has led to the development of embedded computing systems, drawing on principles from electronics, real-time operating systems and software engineering, to create highly versatile automated analytical platforms based on the mentioned principles [15]. In line with these advancements, 3D printing technology has gained significant popularity for prototyping and rapid fabrication of components (e.g., housing, supports, gaskets, and scaffolds) used in the building of affordable automated systems.

This review presents an overview of the applications of various strategies for efficient preparation of environmental samples, focusing on advancements from 2019 to 2024. Specifically, it addresses the development of novel extractive phases based on NMs and the implementation of automated systems for designing new extraction devices and platforms. Selected studies are also highlighted as illustrative examples. Finally, the convergence of nanotechnology and automation in developing sustainable sample preparation approaches is emphasized as a valuable key element, not only from a conceptual standpoint but also from a practical perspective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Occurrence of micropollutants in environmental matrices
ce:section.ce:para: Accurate data on the occurrence of micropollutants, including their measured concentrations and their negative impacts on biological targets, is essential for risk assessment and the evaluation of toxicological potential. In this context, various databases, monitoring projects, and regulatory directives lists, provide valuable information for defining priority actions aimed at pollution prevention and control [16,17]. For example, the NORMAN network, with contributions from members within the European Union (EU) and beyond, has compiled information on more than 120,000 compounds indexed in suspect lists (as of January 2024) relevant for environmental monitoring [18]. According to the surveys and studies, over than 95 % of chemical occurrences are associated with surface water, particularly in river samples, while the remainder is distributed among biota, sediments, and groundwater compartments. Pesticides, pharmaceuticals, personal care products, biocides, plastic additives and flame retardants, among others, are the most prominent classes found in water and groundwater. Consequently, about 1400 compounds have been identified as being substances of very high concern to human health and the environment, based on criteria defined by the EU chemicals legislation [18] (Fig. 1 ). From the dataset, perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) were the most screened, followed by petroleum derivatives, nickel compounds, alkylphenols, aromatic amines, lead compounds, and phthalates (Fig. 1A). Regarding concerns about human health and environment, most of these compounds have been found to be extremely persistent, carcinogenic, mutagenic, and toxic to reproduction, among other harmful effects (Fig. 1B) [19]. Notably, 487 of these substances have been added to the list over the past five years.

As noted, the vast array of compounds, their diverse physicochemical properties, and the inherent complexity of matrices, highlight the need for efficient sample preparation protocols to monitor as many chemicals as possible and investigate their fate in the environment. Several recent contributions in this field will be discussed in the following sections.

Significant progress has been made in recent decades regarding the use of various NMs as alternative extractive phases in SBE technologies for environmental sample preparation [20,21]. Among common NMs, carbonaceous materials (e.g., carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, graphene and carbon dots), inorganic materials (e.g., metal and metal oxides), and metal–organic composites, including metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), have been commonly evaluated as sorbent materials [9]. The outstanding properties of NMs, including size distribution, morphology, chemical composition, and large surface area, play a crucial role in improving the adsorption capability of sorbents toward diverse analytes. In this context, current efforts focus on developing synthetic routes that yield well-defined materials while reducing labor, time, reagent use, and waste compared to traditional physical and chemical methods. To address these challenges, environmentally friendly methods (green synthesis) have emerged as alternative approaches, contributing to minimize environmental and health hazards, in accordance with Green Chemistry principles [22]. Table 1 summarizes several green methods along with their main features, some of which will be discussed in the following sections.

From an experimental perspective, green synthesis is typically carried out in aqueous media or bio-based solvents under controlled temperature and pressure conditions, offering simplicity, energy efficiency, and enhanced safety. Given these advantages, various natural sources, including bacteria, fungi, algal species, fruits, and plant extracts, serve as substrates for the green synthesis of NMs [23–25]. In all cases, the presence of different functional groups and biomolecules influences nanoparticle morphology while also providing notable surface functionality and stability. However, in these routes, the most critical challenges are associated with the mild conditions applied, as well as the availability and inherent variability of natural precursors. Other green methods rely on the use of agricultural and industrial by-products, further contributing to the circular economy and materials recycling [26–28]. Fig. 2 presents a general overview highlighting the strengths and limitations to be considered for green synthesis of NMs nanocomposites.

Even when NMs are obtained through green routes or physical and chemical methods, the synergy between NMs with tailored properties and the broad availability of miniaturized sample preparation approaches can provide simple, fast, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly alternatives to traditional extraction techniques. Fig. 3 summarizes various miniaturized SBE configurations that commonly utilize NMs as sorbents, some of which are discussed in the following sections.

Carbon-based nanomaterials represent a large family of materials composed of carbon allotropes, including carbon nanotubes (CNTs), fullerenes, graphene and derivatives, and carbon dots (CDs). These materials have been considered as green sorbents and attracted much attention due to their high surface area, diverse structural morphology, superior mechanical, and low toxicity [20]. In fact, the combination of two or more interactions in these materials, including hydrophobic surface and π–π conjugate structure, electrostatic interaction, and hydrogen bonding sites, enhances both the stability and selectivity of the extraction phases. Among them CNTs, which are considered tubular structures composed of a monolayer of graphene with sp²-hybridized carbon, have been the most widely used for environmental sample treatment, especially in SBE approaches. Particularly, multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), conceived as nanostructures composed of multiple layers of graphene, have been preferred over single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) due to their higher surface area, which provides more adsorption sites and greater opportunities for functionalization. This, in turn, enhances efficiency and selectivity in extraction procedures. Moreover, MWCNTs are mechanically more stable than SWCNTs, which provide robust sorbents for SPE procedures. In this context, MWCNTs provides tailored sorbents for the extraction and preconcentration of organic micropollutants, such as acetamiprid, atrazine, isoproturon, carbamazepine, diclofenac, antihistaminic and antimigraine drugs, as well as inorganic analytes (e.g. Pb(II), Ni(II), As (III), Cd (II) and Zn(II)) [29–32].

Regarding applications of graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) based materials, these nanostructures were recently proposed for extraction of UV-benzotriazoles and diclofenac in water samples, achieving recovery values between 80.0 and 100 % [33,34]. Considering the high surface area of these graphene-like nanostructures, the main interactions with the organic molecules were assigned to π-stacking, H-bonds and dipole forces interactions. The simplification and miniaturization of the extraction step were assured by directly packing the sorbent into cartridges, which were then used as syringe filters. On the other hand, the application of fullerenes and hydrophobic graphene as sorbents has been quite limited in comparison to CNTs and GO-based materials due to their extreme instability both in aqueous and organic phases, which leads to aggregation and clogging when used in packed devices. Although the provided literature references are limited, several alternatives have been proposed to overcome these issues by following the GSP requirements. For instance, a one-pot green synthesis was developed to obtain magnetic fullerene nanocomposite by using plastic waste (PET bottles) as a feedstock, thereby extending the life-cycle of this material [26]. The prepared material presented relatively high surface area with mesopore/micropore volumes, as well as superparamagnetic properties. This nanocomposite was tested for adsorption of several dyes (e.g., Acid Blue 25 and Methylene Blue) in water, with experimental variables optimized using response surface methodology (RSM). In this context, the experimental design contributed to method development by reducing the number of required experiments. Under optimal conditions, the reusability of the material was tested up to 10 times without losing the extraction efficiency. Overall, the synthesis of the magnetic fullerene nanocomposite was simple, fast, and easy to control, making it suitable for further application in SBE techniques. Similarly, a facile approach was proposed for synthesis of rGO using Tecoma stans leave extracts as reducing/capping agents [23]. This material was proposed as a sorbent for extraction of Ni (II) from water, showing a maximum uptake capacity of 69 mg g-1 under optimized experimental conditions.

Special mention can be made of CDs, a type of “zero-dimensional” quasi-spherical material introduced as novel members of the carbon family in 2004, which possess excellent photoluminescent properties, promoting their extensive use as sensors [35]. Though to a lesser extent, these nanostructures have also been employed as innovative sorbents for the extraction and preconcentration of analytes with diverse properties [27,28,36]. Their enhanced adsorption capacity is primarily attributed to their small size (average size < 10 nm), porous structure, and the presence of multi-functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups. Although in the pioneer study by Xu et al. CDs were obtained during purification of SWCNTs, these nanostructures are now generally synthesized through simple and environmentally friendly routes, using biomass, polysaccharides, proteins, amino acids, simple organic molecules and biopolymers [35,37–39]. Carbon-rich residues and by-products (e.g., soot, carbon black, and combustion residues) can also serve as precursors, thus contributing to the recycling of materials [36]. In all cases, CDs are obtained with excellent chemical stability and tunable surface properties, allowing their incorporation as sorbent in SBE miniaturized modes. As an example, Muckoya et al. proposed the synthesis of CDs by pyrolysis of oat grains at 400 °C for 2 h followed by a washing/centrifugation step to remove large particles and drying in an oven (80 °C, 24 h). The dried powder was packed inside empty SPE cartridges for extraction and preconcentration of parabens and organophosphorus pesticides in wastewater samples prior to determination by LC–MS/MS [27]. The SPE procedure was optimized using a multivariate approach to simultaneously evaluate the variation of experimental factors. Recovery values in the analyzed samples ranged between 62 and 123 % and the reusability of the sorbent was maintained up to five cycles. To illustrate another application, a facile synthesis procedure was followed to generate CDs immobilized on the surface of steel wire using candle soot. In this work, a candle was first ignited to collect approximately 0.2 g of ash. The collected candle ash was transferred into a flask which containing concentrated sulfuric acid and nitric acid (3:1 v/v) to promote the generation of oxygen-containing groups. After sonication treatment, the precipitated mixture was washed and subjected to dialysis to obtain the pure CDs. This material was proposed as SPME coating for efficient and selective determination of phthalate esters (PAEs), exhibiting satisfactory reusability (∼50 times), and good stability. The proposed method was applied to the analysis of five PAEs in real water samples, with recovery values varied between 82 % and 119 % [28]. To date, various composites of CDs and other materials have been applied to enlarge the active surface area of sorbents and balance the hydrophilic property of CDs, resulting in multifunctional sorbents with satisfactory dispersibility for DSPE and MSPE modes [40,41]. For instance, a carbon dots/fatty acid-coated magnetic NPs (CDs/C11-Fe3O4) composite was generated by a facile and simple method for further extraction and determination of benzo[a]pyrene in environmental waters [41]. Herein, CDs were simply obtained by microwave irradiation during 50 min of a citric acid/urea aqueous mixture, followed by filtration with a 0.22 µm membrane. Next, CDs were used for the one-pot synthesis of the magnetic composite. The obtained material was evaluated for adsorption of benzo[a]pyrene, providing satisfactory results in terms of contact time (12 min) and percentage of removal (>90 %) when using 9 mg of material at pH 6. From the mechanistic study, hydrophobic interactions were identified as the driving force behind the adsorption of the analyte. Table 2 provides a summary of SBE techniques based on carbon nanomaterials that have been recently applied for the extraction of environmental pollutants.

Metal oxides nanoparticles (MONPs), mainly those from the transition metals group, are extensively applied in SBE procedures based on the large specific surface area, surface reactivity, and the rapid adsorption and desorption kinetics [42,43]. Additionally, most of the synthetic routes are simple and environmentally friendly [44]. MONPs are often used in combination with other (nano)materials to form hybrid or composite sorbents, aiming to improve adsorption efficiency and selectivity. This expands their application as sorbents not only for inorganic species but also for organic molecules in environmental samples, as recently reported in the literature (Table 3 ) [32,45–48]. Among MONPs, the widespread application of titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanoparticles in SBE techniques is linked to its high chemical stability, cost effectiveness and relative ease of preparation [45]. For instance, the recent applications of green and sustainable synthesis protocols, based on fungi, plant leaves and microwave irradiation have allowed the generation of TiO2NPs with specific characteristics [24,25]. Likewise, CTAB-modified TiO2 nanotubes were proposed as sorbent for SPE of PAHs from tap water and rainwater, achieving extraction yields higher than 80 % [46]. For this, 100 mg of sorbent was packed into empty cartridges and samples were percolated at a specific flow rate. The CTAB-TiO2 sorbent was compared with MWCNTs and C18-silica based SPE, as well as with LLE, showing comparable results to MWCNTs and even better performance than the conventional procedures. On the other hand, zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnONPs) represent another type of nanomaterial with potential applications as sorbents due to their good thermal and chemical stability, electrical conductivity, and high specific surface area, along with simple preparation methods [49]. Hence, environmentally friendly and low-cost procedures allow to obtain non-toxic ZnONPs, with different morphology (e.g., wires, cages, rods, flowers, rings, etc.) accompanied by satisfactory biodegradability and biocompatibility, making them suitable for further application in environmental samples treatment [50]. Though, precise control and optimization of the experimental conditions, such as solvent, reagents, temperature and agitation, are required to generate ZnONPs with uniform size distribution, as well as optimal catalytic and surface properties. Recently, Ag-decorated ZnO nanoflowers were synthesized using a hydrothermal method and subsequently evaluated, for the first time, as a d-µSPE extractant for the extraction and preconcentration of Pb(II) from food and water samples [47]. Results from real samples showed satisfactory performance using only 6 mg of Ag-ZnO NPs, achieving recoveries above 95 % with no significant interference from the analyzed matrices. Overall, the developed d-μSPE procedure demonstrated good accuracy, excellent reproducibility and high sensitivity. Copper oxide-based nanoparticles (CuONPs) have also attracted attention for analytical applications due to the green methods adopted for their synthesis and their outstanding physicochemical properties [48,51]. In this context, an in-tube solid-phase microextraction (IT-SPME) based on a CuONPs-organic silica polymer composite was successfully applied for the extraction of triazine herbicides from environmental water and wastewater samples [48]. It is worth mentioning that, although the synthesis of the nanocomposite requires the use of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and triethoxymethylsilane (MTEOS), two non-green solvents due to their toxicity and emission of volatile by-products, the low quantities used (< 100 µL), along with the resulting waste reduction, aligns with the principles of green approaches. Moreover, the sample only required a filtration step before treatment, thereby maximizing the sample throughput. The authors also proposed a serial coupling of two capillaries, modified with CuONPs and TiO2NPs, respectively, for analysis of compounds with a wide range of polarities. In this approach, the presence of these MONPs enhanced selectivity compared to the unmodified coating, enabling the detection of most herbicides at trace concentration levels. Moreover, the IT-SPME was online coupled to nano-LC, eliminating the need of off-line sample pretreatment and reducing analysis time and waste generation.

Regarding magnetic nanosorbents, magnetic iron oxide-based nanoparticles (MIONPs), like magnetite (Fe3O4) and maghemite (γ-Fe2O3), are commonly studied due to their abundant adsorption sites and large surface-to-volume ratio, which ensure a fast mass transfer. However, their distinctive magnetic properties make them excellent candidates for SBE procedures, enabling the straightforward separation of sorbents from the solution through the use of a magnetic field. This property eliminates the need for filtration or centrifugation during extraction procedures, thus minimizing energy consumption and material usage, in line with the principles of GSP [52,53]. MIONPs have been used as sorbents in magnetic solid-phase extraction (MSPE), a technique that combines the efficiency of magnetic separations with the traditional DSPE approach. Nevertheless, it should be noted that bare MIONPs possess poor dispersibility in aqueous solution due to their tendency to agglomeration, requiring surface functionalization to improve their stability. In this context, monolayers or multilayers of inorganic materials, such as silica, or organic materials, such as activated carbon and polymers, have been employed to prepare functionalized nanoparticles [54,55]. Furthermore, the integration of advanced NMs, including graphene, GO, and CNTs into MIONPs has significantly enhanced their performance in MSPE [56]. In all cases, the selectivity of magnetic sorbents toward targeted organic and inorganic analytes is enhanced by the incorporation of specific functional moieties, along with their high surface areas and satisfactory adsorption capacities. Several examples of the incorporation of functionalized MIONPs into recently developed miniaturized MSPE procedures are provided in Table 4 . For instance, Han et al. proposed the use of magnetic porous carbon (Fe3O4@PC), obtained through different synthesis methods, as sorbents for extraction of organic micropollutants from water samples by MSPE [57,58]. In both studies, the high specific surface area and pore volume of the carbonaceous sorbent played a vital role. For the extraction of triazole fungicides from river water samples, the authors hypothesized that π-π interactions are dominant between the sorbent and the analytes. Experimentally, only 5 mg of sorbent were required to extract the four fungicides from 10 mL of sample in just 5 min, using an oscillator for agitation. Moreover, the Fe3O4@PC could be reused for up to five cycles without a significant loss in extraction capacity, maintaining recoveries above 82 % [58]. In another study, a magnetic molecularly imprinted polymer (MMIP) was prepared using 4‑chloro-2-methylphenoxyacetic acid as template and 4-vinylpiridine as monomer [59]. After appropriate characterization, 50 mg of the composite material were applied for MSPE of five phenoxyacid herbicides from environmental water samples, requiring 1 h for adsorption and 5 min for desorption of the analytes. Under optimal conditions, low limits of detection and quantification were achieved (<2.4 µg L-1), with recoveries ranging from 77 to 98 % for all analyzed samples. Despite the synthesis of the MMIP involved multiple steps and required the use of various reagents and solvents that did not fully adhere to the principles of green synthesis, the sorbent exhibited a remarkable reusability. This characteristic is attributed to its stabilization features, which promote the regeneration over 65 cycles without a loss in extraction capacity. Referring to the determination of PFAS in environmental samples, a recent study highlights the application of magnetic carbon composite nanospheres (MWCNTs@Fe3O4@C) as a sorbent for their extraction through MSPE followed by LC–MS/MS analysis [60]. In this case, the synthesis of the sorbent involved the most effort in terms of time, reagents and energy consumption, while the MSPE procedure only required 10 mL of sample and 44 mg of material to be vortexed for 40 min at room temperature. Moreover, the optimization of experimental conditions for the adsorption and desorption steps was carried out using the Box-Behnken design, significantly reducing the number of experiments involved. Considering the target analytes, which possess a hydrophilic head group and hydrophobic per-fluoroalkyl chains, the high extraction performance was attributed to hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl groups from PFAS and carboxyl moieties of the commercial MWCNTs, along with π-π interactions. Overall, the proposed sorbent provided wide linear ranges (0.1–1000 ng L-1), with low detection limits and good repeatability (3.80–9.52 %). These features make it suitable for the extraction and determination of compounds that are of great concern for human health and the environment.

Notably, a reduced graphene oxide (rGO) modified MIONPs (Fe3O4@rGO) sorbent was proposed for the extraction and preconcentration of five parabens in environmental samples collected from the Antarctic region [61]. Herein, GO was synthesized using the well-established Hummers’ method and subsequently incorporated into the synthesis of magnetite and the in situ generation of rGO. Moreover, an ultrasonication supported (US)-DSPE method was introduced prior to HPLC-UV for trace determination of analytes. Remarkably, the use of ultrasound reduced the contact times during DSPE for both the extraction and elution steps, requiring only 60 s and 10 s, respectively. Accordingly, only 150 µL of acetonitrile were enough for efficient desorption of the target analytes and their subsequent injection into the chromatographic system. This study stands out for requiring minimal amounts of sorbent, samples, and solvents, as well as for the short times involved in the DSPE procedure, contributing to the simplification and miniaturization of the process.

Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) are an appealing family of hybrid porous materials composed of metal clusters and organic ligands, such as polytopic carboxylates compounds. These materials exhibit unique characteristics including high surface area, uniform open cavities, and permanent high porosity with variable shapes and sizes, which make them a foreground platform for sample preparation [62,63]. Depending on the nature of the MOF, diverse interactions can be established with analytes, including hydrophobic or π–π stacking with the ligands, as well as hydrogen bonding sites or dipole–dipole interactions, and also acid–base interactions with metal centers. Among the various synthetic routes, the most commonly adopted have been the nanoprecipitation, solvothermal methods and surfactant-assisted methods. These approaches can involve non-renewable polar solvents, organic compounds, and high boiling conditions. On the other hand, for the production of green MOFs, additional criteria should be considered, such as the selection of cheaper, safer, and/or biocompatible building blocks, low energy requirements, safe reaction media, and straightforward activation processes [64]. In any case, the remarkable high surface area and thermal stability of MOFs have confirmed their suitability as sorbents in SBE techniques for sample preparation, including µSPE, D-µSPE, MSPE and SPME, among others. However, their stability in aqueous environments can be limited, demanding further functionalization strategies in some cases [65]. Table 5 summarizes several recent applications of MOF-based sorbents in environmental sample preparation [66–70]. For instance, a composite of polyacrylonitrile (PAN) nanofibers with highly porous Zn-MOF-74@GO was synthesized using the electrospinning technique and proposed as sorbent material for extraction of chlorobenzenes from water, soil, and food samples [66]. The µSPE cartridge was on-line coupled with HPLC-DAD for the rapid determination of analytes. In this study, the homogeneous distribution of Zn-MOF-74@GO on the surface of the PAN nanofibers provided a sorbent with a favorable structure, associated with an enhancement in the diameters and porosity. Moreover, the large number of functional groups, such as epoxy, hydroxyl, and carboxyl from Zn-MOF-74@GO, facilitated stronger hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic interactions, and π–π stacking between the composite nanofibers and the analytes. Additionally, the nanocomposite demonstrated satisfactory stability in both aqueous and organic media, as well as improved reusability for up to 70 cycles. Although the synthesis of PAN/Zn-MOF-74@GO nanofibers required the use of organic solvents, high temperatures and extended times, the developed analytical method can be considered environmentally friendly due to the minimal amount of sorbent used and the low consumption of organic solvents. In another example, a γCD-MOF composite synthesized using a green and cost-effective method was used to fabricate an SPME fiber for extraction of BTEX (benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene and xylene) via HS-SPME, followed by GC–MS determination [68]. The composite was generated by simply reacting γCD and KOH for 5 days. This prolonged reaction time appeared to be necessary to obtain materials resistant to aqueous environments. The lifetime of the homemade fiber was extended by coating it with a waterproof layer of polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), enabling its reuse more than 300 times. In the study presented by Omarova et al., an in situ solvothermal method was proposed for the generation of a MOF-199-based SPME coating on a stainless-steel substrate [69]. The interest in MOF-199 for the development of SPME coatings stems from its large surface area (1458–1478 m² g-1) and the variable fiber thicknesses that can be achieved. In this context, the effects of solvents and metal salt types, acetic acid addition, dilution factor, and reagents ratio on the synthesis of MOF-199-based SPME coating were studied, considering their impact on the particle size, morphology, surface properties and coating thickness. The proposed method was applied to the quantification of volatile organic compounds, specifically targeting BTEXs in air samples by SPME-GC–MS. High reproducibility (4.5–8.0 %) and repeatability (1.0–6.4 %) were achieved in all cases, with low LOD (0.03–0.09 μg m-3) and LOQ (0.1–0.3 μg m-3). Additionally, the developed fiber demonstrated excellent reusability, maintaining a relative high response over up to 50 uses. By comparing these studies, both MOF-based demonstrated superior performance compared to commercially available fibers for BTEX extraction. The remarkable extraction efficiency is attributed to their large specific surface area and the controlled coating thickness. However, analytes reached extraction equilibrium in just 1 min when using the γCD-MOF-based fiber, whereas it took 10 min with the MOF-199-based fiber. The faster adsorption process observed in the first case may be associated with the crystallographic structure of the material, where the open nanopores of γCD-MOF favored BTEX adsorption.

In another example, a TF-SPME procedure based on MIL-100(Fe) was developed for the extraction of seven personal care products (PCPs) followed by HPLC-PAD [70]. In this sense, three UV filters, three preservatives, and a disinfectant were accurately determined in cosmetics and swimming pool waters. Notably, MIL-100(Fe) was synthesized via a sustainable route, involving precipitation in water at room temperature with 24 h of stirring. The obtained precipitate was incorporated into recycled polystyrene obtained from yogurt containers treated with chloroform. This mixture was used to coat cellulose paper pieces by simply immersion of the support on it, creating a MOF-based TF-SPME device. The MIL-100(Fe) exhibited a particle size ranging from 100 to 500 nm, with well-defined edges in both its neat form and in the TF-SPME configuration. Additionally, the cross-section of the device revealed a composite material layer with a thickness of approximately 210–290 µm. Although various configurations for the PCPs extraction were evaluated, a dynamic extraction mode using vortex agitation for both extraction and desorption was selected for its simplicity, accuracy, and efficiency. Hence, the TF-SPME device demonstrated satisfactory performance when compared to conventional SPE approaches commonly used for these analytes in the tested samples. An interesting aspect of this study was the green assessment of the procedure and its comparison with conventional SPE. The authors performed the evaluation using five metric tools, including the Green Analytical Procedure Index (GAPI), the Complex GAPI (complexGAPI), the Analytical Greenness Metric (AGREE), the AGREE for sample preparation (AGREEprep), and the Sample Preparation Metrics of Sustainability (SPMS). The application of theses metrics was well described, with discussions on the relevant factors considered in each case that may indicate reduced greenness of the method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of automation in sample preparation
ce:section.ce:para: Automation plays a key role in optimizing analytical workflows, as it not only reduces operational responsibilities, but also increases assay performance and reproducibility, while minimizing exposure to hazardous reagents and solvents. In recent years, there has been a notable shift towards modern methods that incorporate miniaturized sample preparation techniques with some degree of automation. This trend reflects an increasing emphasis on green chemistry principles and, more specifically, on GSP guidelines [21,71–73]. Most approaches are increasingly relying on several platforms, assemblies composed of interconnected hardware (e.g., microfluidic devices, robotic modules, flow-based systems, and automated sample handlers) and software that can automate and streamline the sample preparation process. Hence, integrating automation into sample preparation offers several additional advantages, including more efficient and fast procedures that enhance sample throughput [12,74]. Besides, strategies such as on-line and off-line coupling of sample preparation units with detection systems within the same workflow can be implemented to enable real-time or continuous monitoring. Parallelly, the widespread adoption of 3D printing has emerged as a transformative innovation that significantly facilitates the automation and also miniaturization of experimental setups. One of the key advantages of 3D printing lies in its ability to democratize access to advanced analytical technologies. By levering digital templates, researchers worldwide can easily replicate optimized designs, accelerating the development and testing of novel analytical tools. These include the fabrication of separation media (e.g., membranes) and extraction/preconcentration units, chambers or reactors, microfluidic chips, and enclosures for sensing and detection systems [75,76]. Furthermore, the integration of open-source hardware and software (e.g. Fusion 360, LabVIEW, ArduinoIDE, and KiCad), enables the rapid prototyping and fabrication of custom-made devices, and scaffolds tailored to specific applications [77]. The use of open-source microcontrollers, such as Arduino or Raspberry Pi, also offers access to a wide range of user-friendly modules for sensors and actuators, which can be freely adapted from their website (https://www.arduino.cc/ and https://www.raspberrypi.com/) and applied to integrated platforms. As showed in Fig. 4 , these toolkits facilitate the development of diverse lab-made strategies, including platforms for automating sample preparation workflows [15,78].

Flow-based systems are based on the use of interconnected pumps, solenoid valves, commutation and position valves, tubing, mixing/reaction units, and detectors These components facilitate sample processing, including mixing, extraction, purification, separation, and detection. Additionally, these systems enable a precise control over flow rates and timings, while also offering a high degree of automation and flexibility [79]. In this context, flow-based techniques (FTs) have undergone significant evolution over the past decades, resulting in a wide array of analytical methodologies for processing liquid, solid, and gaseous samples [80]. Some of the most widely explored FTs for environmental analysis include sequential-injection analysis (SIA) [81,82], flow-injection analysis (FIA) [83], multi-syringe flow-injection analysis (MSFIA) [76,84,85], lab-in-syringe (LIS) and lab-on-valve (LOV) systems [86,87]. For instance, an interesting study utilized a 3D-printed device for disk-based SPE, developed within an MSFIA system, for the fully automated speciation, extraction and spectrophotometric determination of iron in water samples [76]. To achieve this, a modular device was fabricated using stereolithographic 3D printing to integrate analyte oxidation, disc-based SPE, and complexation with ammonium thiocyanate. This automated configuration replaced typical flow network components, such as tubing and discrete elements, offering improved performance.

More recently, an automatic SIA-UV–vis platform, incorporating a 3D-printed non-supported microelectromembrane extraction (μEME) device, was developed for the determination of methylene blue (MB) in wastewaters [88]. A computer-controlled fluidic system was utilized to perform the steps automatically, which include: i) liquid handling of fluids at the microliter level (up to 44 µL), ii) the formation of extraction phases, iii) in-line extraction of the dye, (iv) flow-through optical detection, and (v) regeneration of the extraction device and flushing of the fluidic lines to restore their functionality. Notably the analytical performance of the 3D-printed fluidic devices with distinct cross-sectional geometries was compared against standard PTFE tubing for automatic non-supported μEME of MB. It was proved that 3D-printed channels with obround cross sections (≤3 mm wide/height) dimensions exhibited the best extraction recovery (up to 60 %) compared to other 3D-printed channels (circular and squared cross-sections) and conventional PTFE tubing (circular cross-sections). In comparison to conventional systems, the proposed automatic SIA-μEME-UV–vis method offers significant improvements in terms of extraction efficiency by reducing the inherent variability of manual handling, minimizing waste generation, and decreasing the use of toxic materials. All these aspects were identified and weighed by the AGREE metric tool showing good adherence to green chemistry principles. Regarding the workflow, the reduced sample preparation steps (only ionic strength adjustment) and the in-line coupling to the detection system are remarkable characteristics supported by automation. However, two critical aspects have to be considered in these kinds of systems. On one hand, the use of organic solvents and photopolymerizable resins for building the 3D fluidic models could affect the sustainability of these devices. On the other hand, the performance of 3D printing technology is often limited by the mechanical reproducibility of filament extrusion and motor control.

On the other hand, Flow-batch (FB) analysis, a hybrid configuration that combines the characteristics of both flow and batch systems, has been notably employed for environmental applications [89–91]. The basic configuration of FB systems generally includes five components: (i) a propulsion system to propel the fluids towards the system, (ii) a transport system composed by tubes and connectors of varied diameters, (iii) a multi-commutation system, mainly constituted by solenoid valves, required for directing the fluids towards the mixing chamber or their recycling to the reservoirs, (iv) a mixing/reaction chamber, where samples are treated and processed with complete control, and (v) a detection unit to monitor a specific property from the analyte(s) and/or reaction products. FB systems leverage the flexibility and versatility of mixing chambers, allowing adaptation to a wide range of sample types and preparation protocols. As an example, an open-source FB platform developed in the laboratory was used for the arsenic extraction from water using the traditional hydride generation (arsine gas) within the system. In this case, the gaseous flow was handled with a microcontroller platform for subsequent detection by a Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM) sensor [90]. Various steps were programmed into the FB system to perform the procedures automatically, avoiding the analyst intervention. The authors also provided step-by-step instructions for the construction and assembly of the FB controller and the whole system, along with the operation instructions. As a proof of concept, the automated methodology was validated in the concentrations range between 10 and 100 µg L-1. More recently, a functional and reliable computer-controlled FB system with a LIS platform was developed for liquid phase microextraction (LPME) of cadmium, copper and lead in water samples [91]. Analytes were in-syringe complexed with ammonium pyrrolidine dithiocarbamate, followed by the extraction with a natural deep eutectic solvent (NADE) for further analysis by flame atomic absorption spectrometry (FAAS). The use of NADES as extraction solvents represented a green alternative to conventional extractants, which supported the greenness of the sample preparation step. Under optimal conditions, the limits of detection ranged between 0.54 and 4.52 μg L-1, and the recoveries were satisfactory when the methodology was applied to the analysis of drinking and environmental water samples. Compared to the flow-based configurations, such as MSFIA and SIA approaches, FB systems stand out for their adaptability and versatility in handling diverse sample types, which is particularly important for environmental analysis. Moreover, FB systems are well-suited for simpler or large-scale processes where high throughput is prioritized. However, MSFIA and SIA offer higher precision and control when handling small reagent volumes and managing simultaneous complex reactions, as these systems incorporate sophisticated equipment with a high degree of automation.

Although not all methodologies based on the aforementioned configurations can be classified as entirely green, since most rely on reagents and solvents, their implementation helps reduce environmental impact by minimizing reagent consumption and waste generation.

Robotic systems were first introduced for analytical applications in the 1980s, and since then, their use has expanded significantly. Nowadays, these machines are widely employed as autosamplers and robotics arms, capable of performing a variety of handling tasks, such as pipetting, mixing, dilution, derivatization, and extraction, among others. Accordingly, higher efficiency, precision, and reliability in sample preparation can be achieved, supported by the ability to execute programmed workflows without the need for human intervention. Currently, a wide range of robotic systems is available, including both commercially produced and lab-fabricated models, with diverse architectures and degrees of complexity and sophistication [15]. For example, a 3D-printed robot, combined with the versatility of an electronic prototyping platform, enabled the development of a lab-made automated system for analysis of drinkable water samples [92]. This robotic system automates the precise manipulation of sample and reagent volumes, enabling multiparametric analyses, such as conductivity, pH, total alkalinity, total hardness, chloride, nitrite, total dissolved phosphorus, and total iron. The robotic system also integrates colorimetric detection through a webcam sensor, thereby simplifying the determination step. Moreover, it streamlines analytical workflows, including dilution, mixing, and titration, while significantly reducing reagent consumption and waste generation. Other remarkable features of this lab-fabricated 3D-printed robotic system include low-cost construction and operation, and easy maintenance. However, despite featuring a user-friendly interface, its dependence on customized hardware could present challenges when applied to the analysis of more complex samples. Incorporating additional units for pre-treatment procedures (e.g., digestion and extraction/preconcentration) may require specific designs for adaptation before integration with the robotic system.

In another study, Qiao et al. described a commercial robotic system integrated with a GC × GC-TOF/MS (gas chromatography coupled with a time-of-flight mass spectrometer), through a custom-designed automation software, which enabled the development of a fully automated workflow for the analysis of organic pollutants in water samples [73]. This system automates every step from water sample collection to pretreatment, including LLE and large-volume injection, prior to online analysis with high-resolution mass spectrometry. Overall, the preparation time for a single sample was approximately 60 min. However, due to the versatility of the robotic workflow, it can simultaneously carry out the pretreatment process for multiple samples, thereby reducing the total analysis time. The obtained results showed that 82.89 % of the 111 analytes passed the validation with satisfactory recoveries (70–120 %). Besides, the platform successfully identified 1902 compounds, including saturated alkanes, aromatics, olefins, alcohols, and epoxides compounds among some others in the analyzed water samples.

These two recent studies highlight the ability of the robotic systems to perform high-throughput and comprehensive analyses of environmental samples. In fact, despite employing different approaches, robotic workflows enabled fully automated water analysis by integrating the sample treatment step with the detection system. Notably, while the 3D-printed robot achieved a sample throughput of over 72 samples per hour, making it a powerful tool for water analysis in routine laboratories and industries, the commercial robotic workflow can operate continuously for 24 h without interruption. This capability not only surpasses the limitations of human working hours but also enables real-time, unmanned monitoring for early warning and traceability of water quality pollutants.

Microfluidic devices, which gained popularity in the early 1990s for analytical applications, have revolutionized the field by offering significant advantages in sample preparation over traditional systems. The most remarkable characteristics include reduced waste generation, improved portability, and the capability for in situ sample treatment, making them highly valuable in environmental analysis [93]. Microfluidic systems consist of structures with width/height dimensions ranging from 100 nm to 100 μm, specifically designed to precisely handle small fluid volumes (from nL to µL) within narrow flow channels using various substrates. Among these, PDMS stands out as the most popular material for fabricating such devices, alongside glass, silicone, and paper, due to its ease of manipulation and low cost. However, PDMS has limitations, such as its tendency to absorb hydrophobic (bio)molecules and solvents (e.g., toluene), leading to polymer swelling. To address this issue, the surface modification and/or functionalization is often required [94,95]. Recently, a straightforward strategy was introduced to deposit metal oxide nano-layers of SiOx, TiOx, and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) inside bonded PDMS microfluidic channels using atmospheric pressure atomic layer deposition (AP-ALD) [96]. This approach enhances the resistance against common organic solvents and addresses the lack of surface anchoring groups required for surface functionalization, thereby broadening the applicability of PDMS-based microchips. Supported by 3D-printing technologies, various droplet microfluidic devices have been developed for the precise and accurate quantification of analytes in environmental matrices [97,98]. These systems not only enable the efficient generation of concentration gradients, maintaining them discrete and stable within microdroplets, but also integrate supportive instrumentation, such as electronics, detection systems and data acquisition tools, including smartphone-based detectors [99]. Hence, automated analytical methods leveraging droplet microfluidics and digital imaging represent a rapidly evolving field. Nowadays, significant efforts are focused on studying hydrodynamics and biphasic flow properties at the microscale, as well as developing multi-commutation techniques for practical applications. For instance, as a proof of concept, microfluidic device was developed for the simple handling of samples and fluorescence detection using a rhodamine probe for mercury determination in coastal waters [100]. The digital microfluidic chip enables the manipulation of liquids as unit-sized discrete micro-droplets, which is achieved through surface-tension modulation induced by an electric field. As showed in Fig. 5 , this system also utilized a feedback control loop to address the challenges posed by diverse ionic compositions in seawater, ensuring reliable droplet actuation during analysis with minimal interference from the sample matrix. Notably, the integrated platform exhibited excellent selectivity and stability under high salinity conditions, enabling mercury detection at the parts-per-billion level within 20 s. Moreover, the entire system is amenable to automation and operates without external hardware (e.g., pumps, tubing, connectors, etc.), making it a sustainable and low-cost approach.

To sum up, microfluidic platforms are transforming the sample preparation step by significantly reducing waste generation, enhancing portability, and enabling in situ treatment. Nevertheless, challenges related to material limitations, process complexity, integration of detection units, and scalability remain bottlenecks to widespread adoption. These factors highlight the need for ongoing optimization to fully harness the potential of microfluidic technologies for analyzing complex environmental samples.

The current trend toward automation, parallel sample processing, and integration with analytical instruments highlights the growing interest in adapting microextraction techniques to achieve high sample throughput. These systems are crucial for meeting the demand for increased efficiency, reduced costs, and the ability to handle large datasets. In this context, analytical methodologies based on headspace systems (HSs) and thin-film microextraction (TFME) have recently gained attention. For instance, HSs have become indispensable in various fields for analysis of volatile and semi-volatile compounds [101,102]. The basic principle involves heating the sample in a sealed container, which causes the volatile compounds to evaporate into the headspace above the sample. The gas phase containing the analytes is then collected for analysis, typically using gas chromatography (GC) and mass spectrometry (MS) [103]. A significant advantage of HSs in environmental sample preparation lies in their ability to simplify complex sample matrices, eliminating the need for labor-intensive extraction processes. In this concern, what makes the HS configuration particularly attractive is its easy adaptability when combined with other extraction techniques, such as headspace solid-phase microextraction (HS-SPME) [104,105] or headspace single-drop microextraction (HS-SDME) [106,107]. These hybrid approaches provide solvent-free and eco-friendly options for trace analysis, aligning with most principles of GAC. Moreover, automated versions, featuring precise control of temperature, volumetric flow, and pressure, along with autosamplers, and integrated data systems, enable high-throughput processing of large sample batches [102,108]. For instance, a gas flow-assisted headspace-single drop microextraction (GF-HS-SDME) method was developed for the extraction, preconcentration, and measurement of BTEX compounds in aqueous samples [109]. This environmentally friendly method minimizes organic solvent use while enhancing extraction efficiency and stability through two innovative strategies: the use of a gas flow to create bubbles that increase the migration rate of analytes, and a bell-shaped tube to generate stable drops. Compared to conventional SDME, the novel bell-shaped tube configuration allows for easier handling and collection of drops, addressing the issue of the extraction phase potentially falling or evaporating. Moreover, the suspended droplets were drawn into a syringe and injected into a GC system for final separation and analysis, thus confirming the compatibility with the analytical technique. The method demonstrated a dynamic linear range between 0.74 and 100.0 μg L-1, LODs ranging from 0.52 to 0.63 μg L-1, and satisfactory recoveries (84.2–102.4 %) in the analyzed samples (drinking water, mineral water and river water).

Another recently developed innovative strategy involves a paper-based sorptive phase (PSP)-TFME method integrated into a high-throughput semi-automated system for extracting endocrine-disrupting compounds from water samples [110]. In this study, the system was designed using 3D printing to facilitate the integration of the PSP within a multi-well plate format. This configuration allowed for the processing of larger sample volumes, ranging from the conventional 2 mL up to 15 mL, while requiring minimal elution solvent (400 µL) to achieve higher enrichment factors. The semi-automated setup provides greater versatility compared to commercially available 96-well plate systems. To generate the PSP, a piece of filter paper (2.5 × 0.5 cm) was simply immersed in a solution containing 20 % (w/v) nylon 66 in formic acid. The filter paper was then placed on a glass surface and left to dry at room temperature to allow solvent evaporation. This process was repeated once to ensure the formation of a thicker layer on the paper surface. The 3D printing process required for the development of the semi-automated 24-well plate system was thoroughly detailed by the authors, with a focus on the individual settings and the assembly of the printed components. The selection of the thermoplastic filaments for 3D printing was particularly relevant in this study, especially for components of the setup exposed to organic solvents during the extraction process. In this context, polyethylene terephthalate glycol (PETG) was selected for printed parts that would not be in contact with solvents, while polypropylene (PP) was suitable for components planned for solvent contact. Under optimized extraction conditions, the enrichment factor was about 15 times. The developed method was successfully applied to the determination of phthalates, (dimethyl phthalate, diethyl phthalate, dibutyl phthalate, benzyl butyl phthalate, bis(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate and di-n-octyl phthalate), alkylphenols (4-octylphenol and 4-nonylphenol) and bisphenols (bisphenol A, bisphenol F) in mineral water and coconut water samples, with LOD values ranging between 1.5 and 7.6 μg L-1. Moreover, the proposed methodology enabled the simultaneous treatment of approximately 24 samples with a processing time of about 8.3 min per sample. Authors concluded that although the sample throughput does not significantly surpass the performance of conventional systems, such as 96-well plates or SPE, the ability to simultaneously process larger sample volumes and the automation capabilities are key aspects to consider.

It is evident that high-throughput extraction systems are becoming increasingly important in the field of environmental analysis, offering rapid, automated, and eco-friendly solutions. In this regard, the integration of advanced detection systems into most of the automated platforms described above provides significant advantages. These include rapid analysis of multiple samples in less time, increased productivity without compromising data quality, and reduced human error by eliminating manual intervention during detection and analysis. Additionally, real-time monitoring and data processing facilitate faster decision-making during analytical workflows. In this concern, systems such as piezoelectric sensors (e.g., Quartz Crystal Microbalance (QCM)) [111], molecular fluorescence [112], and colorimetry (UV–vis or digital images) [113] offer substantial advancements over traditional methods when integrated within automated platforms. As an example, Jiang et al. proposed a HS-SDME procedure for extraction of ammonium in lake, river and wastewater samples using a lab-made automatic FB system, followed by the on-drop conductometric measurement [114]. As showed in Fig. 6 , the FB setup consists of a PP centrifuge tube that functions as the reaction chamber (RC), wherein sample and NaOH solution are pumped using a dual-channel peristaltic pump (PP2), thereby converting ammonium into ammonia by increasing the pH level. Subsequently, ammonia diffuses into the headspace, where reacts with a boric acid drop generated by a syringe pump (SP). Conductivity is measured by an on-drop conductivity probe, which comprises two stainless-steel contacting electrodes (E1 and E2) connected to a circuit board detector (CB). The electronics components are controlled by a microcontroller unit (MCU) and a written program for data acquisition. The sample throughput was estimated to be 10 h-1 and the recovery values were ranged from 99.0 % to 114 % in the analyzed samples. Notably, the proposed platform eliminates the use of harmful or odorous reagents commonly used in conventional methods, such as the Nessler or indophenol blue assays. The proposed HS-SDME procedure significantly reduces reagent consumption, and consequently the waste generation. Additionally, the lab-made FB system demonstrated satisfactory precision and accuracy for the extraction and quantification of ammonium in water samples, with minimal analyst intervention, making the system both environmentally friendly and safe. Unlike conventional methods, this is a membrane-less approach, where the structure of gas-diffusion unit is much simpler than that of membrane-based methods. However, two aspects were identified for further improvement in the extraction system: i) the integration of a contactless conductivity detection unit to prevent electrode corrosion caused by solution contact, which would also reduce the carry-over effect, and ii) the incorporation of a temperature control device, as microextraction performance is influenced by temperature fluctuation. Overall, this system represents a promising alternative for ammonium sensing, offering reduced environmental impact, precision and adaptability for future enhancements.

Similarly, Belén et al. developed an automated computer vision-based analytical method assisted by Multivariate Curve Resolution-Alternating Least Squares (MCR-ALS) for the single-drop determination of arsenic in natural water samples [89]. A lab-made FB system was implemented to facilitate simple and rapid analysis, both essential for routine work. The setup minimizes reagent consumption, requires a reduced sample volume, and results in decreased waste generation. Besides, detection was performed directly on the drop using a digital microscope camera developed as part of the FB system. Experimentally, the setup was performed in a closed reaction chamber, where a drop of silver diethyldithiocarbamate (Ag(DDTC)) solution was suspended at the end of a capillary tube placed inside the chamber. Arsine gas was generated through reduction of arsenic with sodium borohydride at a controlled pH. The gas was then scavenged by the drop, forming a colored trisdiethyldithiocarbamate complex. The color changes within the drop were monitored in situ using colorimetry, and the resulting images were processed with chemometrics tools. In addition, for arsenate determination, the water sample was acidified with a nitric acid solution directly within the FB system, eliminating the need for external sample treatment. The proposed low-cost method exhibits satisfactory performance, with linear response from 0.05 to 1.00 μg L-1 and a LOD of 0.07 μg L-1. Furthermore, this methodology outperformed the standard Ag(DDTC) method in terms of sampling rate, reagents consumption, waste generation, sample volume, and detection mode. Authors demonstrated the enhanced sampling speed and sensitivity of the method when compared to existing methods. However, two aspects require special attention to potentially extend the application to other environmental matrices: on one hand, the maintenance of robust image processing, and on the other, the need for reliable chemometric calibration under variable conditions. In another study, an embedded system based on FB analysis was developed for the detection of arsenic in natural waters, offering a significant advantage by eliminating the use of organic reagents through the implementation of a QCM sensor for detection [90]. Herein, the system used a commercial field-programmable gate array (FPGA) admittance meter for signal processing. The FB setup included two chambers: a reaction chamber where arsine gas is generated during sample treatment, and a detection chamber containing the QCM for real-time measurements. The controller software was developed using the open-source Arduino IDE platform, and all system components were custom designed and 3D printed to ensure adaptability and cost-effectiveness. This embedded FB system represented a cost-effective and environmentally friendly alternative to conventional methods and incorporated significant improvements over the previous study [89]. Main features are associated with the elimination of organic reagents for arsenic isolation and easy integration of a QCM sensor for real-time detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Integration of nanomaterials and automation for high-throughput analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the wide variety of available NMs facilitates their integration into various platforms with a certain degree of automation, establishing a robust framework for environmental sample preparation. As a result, in recent years, several approaches have emerged as leading alternatives, providing significant advancements in terms of sensitivity, selectivity, robustness, reusability, and environmental sustainability. For example, the integration of NMs into analytical systems has significantly enhanced the real-time detection of highly toxic and bioaccumulative compounds, such as lead and mercury at trace levels [115,116]. In the study conducted by Wang et al., an automated headspace-nanoenzyme (HS-NE) sensor was proposed for the determination of mercury ions [116]. Nanoenzymes are NMs with enzyme-like properties that have demonstrated a significant capability to catalyze enzymatic reactions. In this approach, the nanoenzyme-based colorimetric and fluorescent dual sensor enabled both signal transduction and amplification through enzyme-like catalytic processes. The HS-NE sensor was based on HS-SDME configuration and involves the generation of atomic mercury through chemical vapor generation, which then reacts with a droplet of unmodified AuNPs to form Au@HgNPs. A key feature of this system is the strong capability of HS-SDME to separate the target analyte from the sample matrix, combined with the high selectivity achieved through the high-affinity Hg-Au metallophilic interaction. The Au@HgNPs droplet served as catalysts for the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) in the presence of hydrogen peroxide, resulting in the formation of oxidized OPD. The absorbance and fluorescence intensity changes of this product were found to correlate with the concentration of Hg²⁺ present in the sample solution. The proposed methodology achieved LODs of 0.98 nM (colorimetric) and 0.21 nM (fluorescent), both well below the threshold level of 10 nM in drinking water set by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). To facilitate the on-site detection of Hg²⁺, the authors also designed a cost-effective paper-based sensing platform using deposited AuNPs and smartphone-based detection. Under this configuration, Hg2+ detection was achieved in the range of 2–125 nM, with a LOD of 0.27 nM. This study underscores the significance of integrating NMs for miniaturization of sample preparation techniques and improve detection systems for monitoring applications. Likewise, an automated microfluidic concentration gradient colorimetric system was developed for the rapid detection of nitrite in surface water [117]. This system integrates several cutting-edge technologies, including a microfluidic concentration gradient chip, AuNPs for signal amplification, and a custom-built signal acquisition system based on a smartphone application for real-time data analysis. In the designed microfluidic device, the sample preparation process is performed automatically on the chip, eliminating the need for manual dilution or external equipment. AuNPs were then added to each spot to react with the analyte over 20 min. It was observed that as nitrite concentration increased, the intensity of the localized surface plasmon resonance band from AuNPs decreased rapidly, with an evident change in the color of solutions, enabling the monitoring through digital images. In this case, the smartphone app facilitates data conversion and quantitative analysis. Under optimal conditions, the proposed colorimetric method based on AuNPs achieved a lower LOD (0.43 mg L-1) compared to conventional colorimetric methods, while also demonstrating high specificity. The practical application was tested with real water samples, including river and lake water, pure water and tap water, achieving satisfactory recovery values ranging from 94.92 % to 105.60 %.

Regarding the integration of composite materials in automated platforms, an innovative methodology based on hydrophilic-lipophilic modified styrene polymer-magnetite (HLB@Fe3O4) beads was successfully applied to the enrichment of water contaminants [86]. The proposed method followed the flow-based concept of LIS, including automated renewal of the sorbent for each analysis. In fact, the automation of all extraction steps provided satisfactory operational reliability. On the other hand, the incorporation of Fe3O4 nanoparticles allowed the mixing processes, sorbent dispersion, and its recovery in an easy manner. Under optimal conditions, five contaminants, namely mebendazole, bisphenol A, benzyl 4-hydroxybenzoate, diclofenac, and triclosan, were extracted from 3.5 mL of surface water using MSPE and determined by on-line coupling of the sample treatment unit to HPLC with spectrometric detection. The LODs were ranged from 1.2 μg L-1 to 6.5 μg L-1 with an RSD value less than 7 % for all the analytes, and recoveries ranged from 78.4 % to 105.6 %. The high versatility of MIONPs has also been demonstrated for their incorporation in microfluidic chips for extraction of five PAHs in water samples, followed by subsequent determination using GC–MS [118]. The on-chip MSPE required only 2 mL of sample, 2 mg of extractant, and 50 μL of elution organic solvent, being environmentally friendly and consistent with the GSP guidelines. Moreover, the sample loading, washing and elution steps were easily implemented with microinjection pumps, which significantly reduce manual operation. Under optimized conditions, the on-chip-MSPE-GC–MS method provided LODs between 0.08 – 0.26 µg L-1 with recovery values ranged between 89.9 and 113.7 %. A wider comparison between these two MIONPs-based methods shows that the inclusion of minimal amounts of magnetic sorbents enables the straightforward extraction of organic pollutants from water samples using different setups. Additionally, both analytical platforms reduce manual operation and handling of samples, extractants and solvents, as well as facilitate integration with chromatographic techniques. Particularly, the proposed on-chip MSPE procedure proved to be cost-effective and environmentally friendly, given its simple design, easy manufacturing, and low requirements for sample and elution solvent volumes.

In another example, a composite of zero-valent iron nanoparticles (nZVI) supported on reduced graphene oxide (rGO) was proposed for the modification of a QCM within an automated FB system for determination of total arsenic (As) in water samples [119]. The setup was divided in two steps, where the arsine gas was firstly generated from As (As3+ and/or As5+) in a reaction chamber employing minimal quantities of reducing reagent, and then delivered to a 3D-fabricated sensing chamber containing the modified QCM sensor (Fig. 7 ). The study demonstrated that the nZVI/rGO-modified sensor exhibited superior sensitivity, precision, and reproducibility compared to a sensor without nanoparticles. This enhancement was primarily attributed to the increased active surface area provided by the nanocomposite, which facilitates a stronger and more selective interaction with arsine. Additionally, the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups contributes significantly to the improved adsorption capacity and chemical reactivity, further enhancing the sensor's performance. Moreover, the nZVI/rGO nanosorbent showed good reusability, with an extended use up to 91 cycles, and with RSD values ranged between 4 % and 5 %. Under optimized conditions, a LOD of 0.0062 µg L-1 was achieved with a sample rate of 7 h-1. It is worth noting that the inclusion of the nZVI/rGO nanocomposite for arsenic adsorption onto the QCM surface significantly improves the sensitivity of the method compared to previous studies [89,90]. Moreover, the low sample volumes required, the absence of organic reagents, and the reusability of the nanocomposite support the environmental friendliness of the proposed methodology.

In light of the significant advances achieved so far, the integration of NMs into automated platforms is driving the development of high-throughput systems, enhancing environmental sample preparation and analysis. Table 6 summarizes the main characteristics of the aforementioned studies. In all these methodologies, the concept of “green approach” has been mentioned through key aspects such as the toxicity of solvents used, reagents, energy consumption, and waste generation, but it has not been explicitly assessed using metric tools. However, a standardized evaluation of environmental impact could be conducted using various metric tools. A comprehensive overview of the tools currently available for assessing greenness metrics can be found in the literature [120–122]. Among them, AGREEprep focuses on sample preparation by evaluating criteria such as solvent selection, reagent use, waste generation, energy consumption, sample size, and throughput, primarily based on the ten principles of GSP. These criteria cover various aspects contributing to the overall greenness of sample preparation. Distinctively, the assessment using AGREEprep is easy to perform through open-access software (available at mostwiedzy.pl/AGREEprep). A visual representation of greenness is provided through a circular scale (0 to 1), where higher scores indicate more environmentally friendly approaches. Complementary, the SPMS metric proposed by González Martín et al. in 2023, exclusively evaluates and compares extraction techniques, without considering any aspect related to sampling or final detection technique, as is the case with other metric tools.

Modern sample preparation methods are expected to be eco-friendly while also prioritizing simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and time efficiency. These qualities are essential when analyzing complex matrices or processing large numbers of samples, as is often required in environmental analysis. To meet these demands, the integration of various nanomaterials and automation into high-throughput systems have emerged as key drivers of innovation in developing more sustainable approaches. Consequently, the synergy between nanotechnology and automation supported by innovative tools, such as 3D printing and open-source software, has the potential to redefine the core principles of modern sample preparation, emphasizing simplification, sustainability, and affordability. However, despite these promising advances, their full integration for environmental sample preparation remains an emerging and relatively underexplored field. The limited number of studies highlights the challenges involved in designing and implementing such systems. These challenges mainly include the precise control of nanomaterial synthesis, ensuring stability and reproducibility under real-world conditions, and achieving satisfactory automation for high-throughput applications. Future advancements will require multidisciplinary collaboration to address existing limitations, particularly those related to remote monitoring and real-time data acquisition. These efforts will not only advance the fields of sample preparation and high-throughput method development but will also make significant contributions to addressing pressing environmental challenges. In this context, optimizing methods and assessing the green profile of analytical applications is an essential global strategy.

Federico Belén: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Julián Gutiérrez: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation.
